title: Understanding LLMs by Building One
tagline: Build a GPT from scratch, one concept at a time, from pure Python to production serving.

chapters.title: Foundations
chapters.description:
Every deep learning framework hides the same core loop: forward pass → loss → backward pass → update. These two labs strip away all abstraction so you see exactly what happens.

Lab 01 implements Karpathy's original microGPT — a complete GPT in pure Python with a hand-built scalar autograd engine. Every gradient flows through a graph you can inspect. Lab 02 replaces scalars with NumPy arrays for speed, but still derives every gradient by hand. Together they answer: what is a neural network actually computing?

Start here if you're new to transformers, or if you want to truly understand what loss.backward() does under the hood.
chapters.diagram:
graph TD
  A[Input] --> B[Forward pass]
  B --> C[Loss]
  C --> D[Backward pass]
  D --> E[Update weights]
  E --> F[Next step]

knowledge_checks.question: In the pure Python microGPT, what does the Value class primarily track?
options: The data types of each variable
A computation graph for automatic differentiation
Memory allocation for tensors
The learning rate schedule
knowledge_checks.explanation: The Value class wraps each scalar and records every operation (add, multiply, etc.) in a computation graph. This graph is then traversed in reverse during the backward pass to compute gradients via the chain rule — this is the essence of automatic differentiation.

knowledge_checks.question: Why does Lab 02 derive every gradient by hand instead of using autograd?
options: Hand-derived gradients are always more accurate than autograd
It forces you to understand the chain rule applied to real matrix operations
NumPy does not support any form of automatic differentiation
Manual gradients run faster than autograd in all cases
knowledge_checks.explanation: Deriving gradients manually for matrix multiplications, softmax, and RMSNorm builds deep understanding of what autograd does automatically. Once you have computed dL/dX by hand for each layer, frameworks like PyTorch feel like a natural shortcut rather than a black box.

knowledge_checks.question: What is the fundamental loop that both foundation labs implement?
options: Load data, tokenize, save model
Forward pass, compute loss, backward pass, update weights
Encode input, decode output, evaluate accuracy
Initialize weights, freeze layers, fine-tune
knowledge_checks.explanation: Both labs implement the core training loop: a forward pass computes predictions, the loss measures how wrong they are, the backward pass computes gradients of the loss with respect to every parameter, and the update step adjusts parameters to reduce the loss. Every deep learning framework hides this same loop.

chapters.title: Frameworks
chapters.description:
The same transformer architecture expressed in three different frameworks — PyTorch, JAX, and MLX — each with a single-example and batched variant.

PyTorch is imperative and object-oriented: you define nn.Module classes and call .backward(). JAX is purely functional: no classes, no mutation, explicit random keys, and jax.vmap to auto-vectorize. MLX targets Apple Silicon with unified CPU/GPU memory and lazy evaluation.

Comparing these side-by-side reveals what's essential to the algorithm vs. what's just framework convention. The batched variants add padding, masking, and mini-batch training — the bridge from educational toy to real training.
chapters.diagram:
graph TD
  A[Same transformer] --> B[PyTorch]
  B --> C[nn.Module + backward]
  C --> D[JAX]
  D --> E[Pure functions + grad]
  E --> F[MLX]
  F --> G[Lazy eval + Apple GPU]
  G --> H[Compare implementations]

knowledge_checks.question: What is the key difference between PyTorch's and JAX's approach to computing gradients?
options: PyTorch is faster; JAX is slower
PyTorch uses .backward() on a loss tensor; JAX transforms a function with jax.grad
PyTorch requires manual gradients; JAX uses autograd
PyTorch only works on GPU; JAX works on CPU
knowledge_checks.explanation: PyTorch's imperative style calls loss.backward() to trace the computation graph and fill .grad attributes on parameters. JAX's functional style uses jax.grad to transform a pure function into one that returns gradients. Both compute the same thing — the chain rule — but with fundamentally different programming paradigms.

knowledge_checks.question: What does jax.vmap accomplish that would otherwise require manual code changes?
options: It compiles Python to machine code
It distributes training across multiple GPUs
It automatically vectorizes single-example code to process batches
It converts PyTorch models to JAX format
knowledge_checks.explanation: jax.vmap takes a function written for a single example and automatically adds a batch dimension, vectorizing it to process many examples simultaneously. Without vmap, you would need to manually reshape tensors and add batch dimensions throughout your code, as done in the PyTorch batched lab.

knowledge_checks.question: Why does MLX use lazy evaluation instead of executing operations immediately?
options: To save disk space when storing models
To enable operation fusion and optimize memory access patterns before execution
Because Apple Silicon cannot execute eager operations
To make debugging easier with step-by-step traces
knowledge_checks.explanation: Lazy evaluation builds a computation graph first, then optimizes and executes it when mx.eval() is called. This allows MLX to fuse operations (combining multiple steps into one kernel), plan memory usage efficiently, and optimize access patterns for Apple Silicon's unified memory architecture.

chapters.title: Tokenization & Architecture
chapters.description:
Modern LLMs differ from the original transformer in three key ways: how they tokenize text, encode position, and share attention heads. These labs implement each upgrade from scratch.

BPE tokenization (Lab 09) replaces character-level input with learned subword units — the same algorithm behind GPT's tokenizer. RoPE (Lab 10) encodes position by rotating query and key vectors in complex space, enabling length generalization. GQA (Lab 11) shares key-value heads across query heads, cutting memory without losing quality.

These are the building blocks of LLaMA, Mistral, and every modern open-weight model.
chapters.diagram:
graph TD
  A[Raw text] --> B[BPE merges]
  B --> C[Token IDs]
  C --> D[Token embeddings]
  D --> E[RoPE rotates Q and K]
  E --> F[GQA shares KV heads]
  F --> G[Modern transformer input pipeline]

knowledge_checks.question: How does BPE tokenization balance vocabulary size against sequence length?
options: It uses a fixed dictionary of English words
It iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent character pairs into new tokens
It splits every word into individual characters regardless of frequency
It assigns one token per sentence
knowledge_checks.explanation: BPE starts with individual characters and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a single new token. Common words become single tokens (short sequences) while rare words stay split into subword pieces. More merges mean larger vocabulary but shorter sequences — this is the fundamental tradeoff.

knowledge_checks.question: Why does RoPE encode position by rotating Q and K vectors rather than adding a position embedding?
options: Rotation is computationally cheaper than addition
The dot product of rotated vectors depends only on relative position, enabling length generalization
Addition causes numerical overflow with long sequences
Rotation produces more visually interpretable attention maps
knowledge_checks.explanation: When Q and K vectors are rotated by position-dependent angles, their dot product (which determines attention weights) depends only on the difference between their positions, not their absolute positions. This means a model trained on sequences of length 512 can generalize to length 2048 without retraining.

knowledge_checks.question: In Grouped-Query Attention, what is shared across multiple query heads?
options: The output projection weights
The query projection weights
The key and value heads
The embedding layer
knowledge_checks.explanation: GQA reduces memory by having groups of query heads share the same key-value head. Standard MHA gives each query its own KV head, MQA uses just one KV head for all queries, and GQA sits in between. This directly reduces the KV cache size during inference, which is the primary memory bottleneck.

chapters.title: Inference Optimization
chapters.description:
Autoregressive generation is inherently sequential: each token depends on all previous tokens. These labs tackle the two fundamental questions: how to avoid redundant computation and how to control output quality.

The KV cache (Lab 12) is the single most important inference optimization — it stores previously computed key-value pairs so each new token only needs one matrix multiply instead of reprocessing the entire sequence. Sampling strategies (Lab 13) show how temperature, top-k, top-p, and min-p produce completely different outputs from the same model.

Every production LLM serving system uses both of these techniques.
chapters.diagram:
graph TD
  A[Prompt prefix] --> B[KV cache reuse]
  B --> C[Next-token logits]
  C --> D{Sampling rule}
  D --> E[Greedy]
  D --> F[Top-k / top-p / min-p]
  E --> G[Next token]
  F --> G
  G --> B

knowledge_checks.question: What computational redundancy does the KV cache eliminate during autoregressive generation?
options: It avoids recomputing the embedding layer for each token
It avoids recomputing key and value projections for all previous tokens at each step
It caches the final output probabilities to skip softmax
It stores the loss values to avoid recomputing them
knowledge_checks.explanation: Without a KV cache, generating each new token requires recomputing the key and value projections for the entire sequence from scratch. The KV cache stores previously computed K and V tensors and only computes the projection for the new token, then concatenates it with the cache. This reduces per-step projection work from O(n) to O(1).

knowledge_checks.question: Why does the same model produce completely different outputs with greedy sampling vs. top-p sampling?
options: They use different model weights
Greedy always picks the highest-probability token; top-p samples from a truncated distribution, introducing diversity
Top-p modifies the model architecture at inference time
Greedy sampling uses a different tokenizer
knowledge_checks.explanation: Greedy sampling deterministically selects the most probable token at every step, producing repetitive and predictable output. Top-p (nucleus) sampling considers all tokens whose cumulative probability reaches a threshold p, then samples from that set. This controlled randomness produces diverse, creative output while avoiding very unlikely tokens.

chapters.title: Fine-tuning & Deployment
chapters.description:
Training a model from scratch is expensive. These labs show how to adapt and compress existing models efficiently.

LoRA (Lab 14) freezes the original model weights and injects tiny low-rank matrices that capture task-specific knowledge — training only ~1% of parameters while matching full fine-tuning quality. Quantization (Lab 15) compresses a trained model from 32-bit floats to 8-bit integers, reducing size by 4×. This educational implementation demonstrates the memory savings directly and explains why production INT8 kernels also improve latency.

Together they answer: how do you take a large pretrained model and make it yours, then make it fast?
chapters.diagram:
graph TD
  A[Base model] --> B[LoRA adapters]
  A --> C[Post-training quantization]
  B --> D[Merge or keep adapters]
  C --> E[Smaller weights]
  D --> F[Deployable model]
  E --> F

knowledge_checks.question: How does LoRA achieve comparable quality to full fine-tuning with far fewer trainable parameters?
options: It prunes unimportant weights from the model
It injects small low-rank matrices alongside frozen weights that capture task-specific adaptations
It trains only the final output layer while freezing everything else
It uses knowledge distillation from a larger model
knowledge_checks.explanation: LoRA freezes the original weight matrix W and adds a low-rank decomposition BA (where B and A have much smaller inner dimensions). The adaptation is learned in this compressed space, typically using only ~1% of the original parameters. At inference, W + BA can be merged into a single matrix with zero overhead.

knowledge_checks.question: What happens to model output quality when you quantize weights from FP32 to INT8?
options: Output quality is always identical since INT8 is lossless
Output quality degrades significantly for all models
Output quality degrades slightly because quantization introduces small rounding errors
The model cannot run at all after quantization
knowledge_checks.explanation: INT8 quantization maps continuous float values to 256 discrete levels, introducing small rounding errors. Because neural network weights tend to cluster near zero and the quantization grid covers the full weight range, these errors are typically small. Most models show negligible quality loss, making the 4x size reduction worthwhile for production deployment.

chapters.title: Alternative Paradigms
chapters.description:
Not all language models generate left-to-right. These labs explore fundamentally different approaches to text generation.

Text diffusion (Lab 16) starts from pure noise — all [MASK] tokens — and iteratively unmasks them, like image diffusion but for text. Soft thinking (Lab 17) preserves the full output distribution instead of collapsing to a single token, passing probability-weighted "concept tokens" forward. Soft training (Lab 18) closes the train-test gap by teaching the model to work with its own uncertain outputs during training.

These represent active research frontiers in how we think about language generation.
chapters.diagram:
graph TD A[Text generation] --> B[Autoregressive] B --> B2[One next token
at a time] B2 --> C[Diffusion] C --> C2[Predict masked positions
in parallel] C2 --> D[Soft tokens] D --> D2[Preserve uncertainty
as embeddings]

knowledge_checks.question: How does text diffusion differ fundamentally from autoregressive generation?
options: It generates tokens faster by skipping the attention mechanism
It starts from all-masked tokens and iteratively unmasks by confidence, rather than generating left-to-right
It uses a different loss function but the same generation order
It requires a much larger model to work correctly
knowledge_checks.explanation: Autoregressive models generate strictly left-to-right, one token at a time. Text diffusion starts from a fully masked sequence and reveals tokens in order of model confidence — it might unmask the middle of a word before the beginning. This is analogous to how image diffusion models refine from noise to a clear image.

knowledge_checks.question: What problem does soft training solve that soft thinking alone does not?
options: Soft thinking is too slow for real-time inference
Soft thinking creates a train-test mismatch because the model trains on hard tokens but infers with soft tokens
Soft thinking does not support batched training
Soft thinking cannot work with BPE tokenization
knowledge_checks.explanation: Soft thinking uses probability-weighted concept tokens at inference but trains with standard one-hot tokens. The model never sees blended inputs during training, creating a distribution mismatch. Soft training fixes this with a curriculum that gradually transitions from hard to soft inputs during training, so the model learns to interpret its own uncertain outputs.

knowledge_checks.question: Why is a curriculum schedule essential for soft training to work?
options: Without a schedule, the GPU runs out of memory
Early model predictions are random noise, so starting with all-soft inputs provides no useful training signal
The curriculum is optional and only affects training speed
A fixed schedule is required by the PyTorch optimizer
knowledge_checks.explanation: At the start of training, the model's output distribution is essentially random, so concept tokens computed from it are meaningless noise. The curriculum starts with hard (one-hot) tokens that provide clear training signal, then gradually increases the proportion of soft tokens as the model improves and its predictions become meaningful.

chapters.title: Production Serving
chapters.description:
Serving LLMs at scale requires solving memory, latency, and throughput problems simultaneously. These labs implement the key algorithms behind vLLM, FlashAttention, and TensorRT-LLM.

Speculative decoding (Lab 19) uses a small draft model to guess ahead while a larger model verifies in one pass — lossless speedup. FlashAttention (Lab 20) restructures attention to stay in fast on-chip SRAM, avoiding the memory wall. PagedAttention (Lab 21) applies OS-style virtual memory paging to KV caches, eliminating fragmentation. Disaggregated serving (Lab 22) splits prefill and decode onto separate workers, removing head-of-line blocking.

These are the techniques that make it possible to serve millions of concurrent LLM requests.
chapters.diagram:
graph TD
  A[Serving stack] --> B[Speculative decoding]
  B --> B2[Fewer target passes]
  B2 --> C[FlashAttention]
  C --> C2[Less attention traffic]
  C2 --> D[PagedAttention]
  D --> D2[Better KV utilization]
  D2 --> E[Disaggregated serving]
  E --> E2[Separate prefill and decode]
  E2 --> F[Higher throughput]

knowledge_checks.question: Why is speculative decoding considered a 'lossless' speedup technique?
options: It compresses the model weights without any loss
The verification step guarantees the output distribution is mathematically identical to the target model alone
It uses lossless data compression for the KV cache
It skips attention layers that contribute less than a threshold
knowledge_checks.explanation: The draft model proposes tokens and the target model verifies them in a single forward pass. When a draft token is accepted, it matches what the target would have generated. When rejected, the target model's own token is used instead. This accept-reject scheme provably produces the exact same output distribution as running the target model alone — just faster.

knowledge_checks.question: What is the core insight behind FlashAttention's memory efficiency?
options: It uses lower precision arithmetic to reduce memory usage
It processes attention in tiles that fit in fast on-chip SRAM, avoiding materializing the full N*N matrix in slow HBM
It approximates attention with a sparse matrix to save memory
It computes attention only for the most important token pairs
knowledge_checks.explanation: Standard attention computes and stores the full N*N attention matrix in HBM (GPU main memory), requiring O(N^2) memory. FlashAttention breaks this into small tiles processed in fast SRAM, using the online softmax (log-sum-exp) trick to accumulate results without ever materializing the full matrix. The output is mathematically identical but memory usage drops to O(N).

knowledge_checks.question: Why does disaggregated serving split prefill and decode onto separate workers?
options: Prefill and decode use incompatible hardware
Prefill is compute-bound and decode is memory-bound; mixing them on one GPU causes head-of-line blocking
It is required by the HTTP protocol for streaming responses
Decode workers need more CPU cores than prefill workers
knowledge_checks.explanation: Prefill processes the entire prompt at once (compute-bound, high throughput), while decode generates tokens one at a time (memory-bound, latency-sensitive). When both run on the same GPU, a long prefill blocks short decode requests, causing latency spikes. Splitting them onto separate workers eliminates this interference and allows independent scaling of each phase.

chapters.title: Self-Improvement
chapters.description:
Can a model make itself better? These labs explore two fundamentally different approaches to self-improvement — one of the most active frontiers in AI research.

Lab 23 improves model outputs: generate candidates, score them with a verifiable quality function, keep the best, retrain. This is the pattern behind STaR, self-rewarding language models, and Karpathy's autoresearch. Lab 24 improves the model itself: maintain a population of model variants with different hyperparameters, select the fittest, mutate and evolve. This is Population Based Training — the same evolutionary loop behind FunSearch and neural architecture search.

Together they cover both axes of self-improvement: better data (what the model learns from) and better architecture (how the model is structured).
chapters.diagram:
graph TD A[Model improvement] --> B[Improve outputs] A --> C[Improve configurations] B --> D[Generate
score
filter
retrain] C --> E[Population
evaluate
select
mutate] D --> F[Better samples] E --> G[Better models]

knowledge_checks.question: Why does the self-improvement loop in Lab 23 filter generated outputs before retraining?
options: To reduce the amount of training data for faster iteration
Keeping all self-generated outputs causes the model to amplify its own errors, leading to collapse
Filtering is optional and only affects training speed
The model cannot process more than a fixed number of training examples
knowledge_checks.explanation: Without filtering, the model retrains on its own mistakes — including low-quality outputs — which amplifies errors across rounds. This is called self-training collapse. The quality filter ensures only verified good outputs enter the training set, following the same principle as STaR (keep only correct rationales) and autoresearch (keep only changes that improve loss).

knowledge_checks.question: What is the key advantage of population-based training over training a single model with random hyperparameters?
options: It uses less total compute
It combines exploration (mutations) with exploitation (inheriting successful weights), discovering good configurations faster
It guarantees finding the optimal hyperparameters
It does not require a validation set
knowledge_checks.explanation: PBT maintains a population where the best configurations survive and reproduce. Children inherit trained weights from successful parents (exploit) while exploring new hyperparameter settings (explore). This means good discoveries are immediately shared across the population, rather than being locked into a single training run. A random single run might hit a bad configuration and waste all its compute.

knowledge_checks.question: How do Labs 23 and 24 differ in what they improve?
options: Lab 23 improves speed; Lab 24 improves accuracy
Lab 23 improves the training data (model outputs); Lab 24 improves the model itself (architecture/hyperparameters)
Lab 23 uses supervised learning; Lab 24 uses reinforcement learning
Lab 23 works on small models; Lab 24 only works on large models
knowledge_checks.explanation: Lab 23 (STaR-style) keeps the model architecture fixed and improves what it learns from — the model generates better training data for itself. Lab 24 (PBT-style) keeps the training data fixed and improves how the model is structured — evolving hyperparameters like learning rate, embedding dimension, and number of layers. Together they cover both axes of self-improvement.

labs.title: microGPT, Pure Python
labs.tagline: Karpathy's original microGPT, unmodified except for the dataset path. The complete algorithm in one file.
labs.description:
This is where it all starts — Andrej Karpathy's microGPT, a complete GPT implementation in a single file of pure Python with zero dependencies.

The code builds a scalar autograd engine (the Value class), implements a character-level transformer with multi-head attention, RMSNorm, and a linear learning rate schedule, then trains it on ~32,000 human names. Every operation — every multiply, every addition — is tracked in a computation graph so gradients can flow backward automatically.

This is the most educational implementation: you can literally trace every number through the network. It's also the slowest, because Python loops over individual scalars. The next labs will show how frameworks eliminate that overhead while keeping the same algorithm.
learning_objectives: Trace scalar values through a computation graph
Explain how automatic differentiation computes gradients
Identify the core components of a transformer architecture

exercises.prompt: Try changing the number of attention heads in the model and observe how it affects the generated names. What happens with 1 head vs. 4 heads?
exercises.hint: Look for the n_head parameter near the top of the file. Run the model with different values and compare the output after the same number of training steps.

exercises.prompt: Add a print statement inside the backward pass of the Value class to trace how gradients flow through a single addition operation. What order are the gradients computed in?
exercises.hint: Find the _backward closure defined inside __add__. Print self.grad before and after the backward call to see the chain rule in action.

labs.title: NumPy with Manual Backpropagation
labs.tagline: Same architecture, but NumPy handles the math. Every gradient is derived by hand.
labs.description:
This lab replaces Python scalars with NumPy arrays, making the forward pass fast — but keeps every gradient derived by hand in the backward pass.

You'll see the chain rule applied explicitly to matrix multiplications, softmax, RMSNorm, and cross-entropy loss. Each backward function computes dL/dX given dL/dY from the layer above. This is exactly what PyTorch's autograd does automatically, but here you can read every line.

The key insight: the forward pass is just linear algebra, and the backward pass is just more linear algebra applied in reverse order. Once you see it done manually, automatic differentiation feels like a natural shortcut rather than magic.
learning_objectives: Derive gradients for matrix operations using the chain rule
Implement backward passes for softmax, RMSNorm, and cross-entropy
Compare scalar autograd overhead vs. vectorized NumPy operations

exercises.prompt: Comment out the RMSNorm backward pass and replace it with a numerical gradient check using finite differences. Do the gradients match?
exercises.hint: For each parameter p, compute (loss(p+eps) - loss(p-eps)) / (2*eps) with eps=1e-5 and compare to the analytical gradient.

exercises.prompt: Swap the order of the backward passes for two consecutive layers. What happens to the gradients and why?
exercises.hint: The chain rule requires processing layers in reverse order. Swapping two layers means one layer receives the wrong upstream gradient.

labs.title: PyTorch
labs.tagline: PyTorch handles tensors and gradients. See how much boilerplate disappears.
labs.description:
The same transformer architecture, now in PyTorch. Autograd handles all gradients automatically — no more manual backward passes.

The model is defined as nn.Module subclasses with learnable nn.Parameter tensors. Call loss.backward() and PyTorch traces the computation graph, computes all gradients, and stores them in .grad attributes. Compare this to Lab 02 to see exactly how much boilerplate disappears.

This is the imperative, object-oriented style that most ML practitioners use daily. The next lab adds batching to bridge the gap to real training.
learning_objectives: Implement a transformer using PyTorch nn.Module and autograd
Explain how loss.backward() eliminates manual gradient code
Compare PyTorch's imperative style with manual backpropagation

exercises.prompt: Register a backward hook on one of the attention layers to print gradient norms at each training step. Do they grow, shrink, or stay stable?
exercises.hint: Use module.register_full_backward_hook(fn) where fn receives the module, grad_input, and grad_output. Print torch.norm(grad_output[0]).

exercises.prompt: Change the optimizer from the current one to plain SGD (no momentum). How many more training steps does it need to reach the same loss?
exercises.hint: Replace the optimizer with torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=...). You may need to adjust the learning rate significantly.

labs.title: PyTorch Batched
labs.tagline: Mini-batch training bridges the gap between educational toy and real training.
labs.description:
Real training doesn't process one example at a time — it uses mini-batches. This lab adds padding, attention masking, and batch-level loss computation to the PyTorch model.

Names have different lengths, so shorter ones are padded with a special token. The attention mask prevents the model from attending to padding positions. Loss is computed only on real tokens, not padding. These are the same techniques used in production training of billion-parameter models.

The batch size of 32 means 32× more work per gradient step, but the gradient is also 32× less noisy. This is the fundamental tradeoff of mini-batch SGD.
learning_objectives: Implement padding and attention masking for variable-length sequences
Explain the tradeoff between batch size and gradient noise
Design a mini-batch training loop with proper loss masking

exercises.prompt: Change the batch size from 32 to 1 and then to 128. Plot the loss curves for all three. Which converges fastest in wall-clock time?
exercises.hint: Larger batches give smoother gradients but fewer updates per second. Track both loss and elapsed time to compare fairly.

exercises.prompt: Remove the attention mask so the model can attend to padding tokens. How does the generated output change after training?
exercises.hint: Look for where the mask is applied in the attention computation. Set it to None or all-ones and observe the quality of generated names.

labs.title: JAX
labs.tagline: Purely functional style. No classes, no hidden state, no mutation.
labs.description:
JAX takes a radically different approach: everything is a pure function. No classes, no mutable state, no .backward() method.

Model parameters are plain dictionaries of arrays. The forward pass is a function that takes parameters and data, returns a loss. jax.grad transforms that function into one that returns gradients. jax.jit compiles it for speed. Random numbers require explicit keys that must be split — no hidden global state.

This functional style makes it trivial to compose transformations: differentiate, vectorize, parallelize, compile — all by wrapping functions.
learning_objectives: Implement a transformer using pure functions without mutable state
Apply jax.grad and jax.jit to transform and compile functions
Compare functional and object-oriented ML programming paradigms

exercises.prompt: Remove the @jax.jit decorator from the training step function. Measure how much slower each step becomes without JIT compilation.
exercises.hint: Use time.time() around the training step. JIT compilation has a one-time cost on the first call, so compare from the second call onward.

exercises.prompt: Try reusing a random key instead of splitting it at each step. What goes wrong with the generated output and why?
exercises.hint: JAX random keys are deterministic. Reusing the same key means every random operation produces identical results, breaking sampling diversity.

labs.title: JAX Batched
labs.tagline: Use jax.vmap to automatically vectorize single-example code across a batch.
labs.description:
jax.vmap is JAX's killer feature for batching: write code for a single example, and vmap automatically vectorizes it across a batch dimension.

Instead of manually reshaping tensors and adding batch dimensions everywhere (like in PyTorch batched), you write the clean single-example version and let the compiler figure out the batching. The result is identical to hand-batched code but with zero manual effort.

This lab shows the same padding and masking as PyTorch batched, but the core model code stays single-example clean.
learning_objectives: Apply jax.vmap to auto-vectorize single-example code across batches
Compare manual batching (PyTorch) with automatic vectorization (JAX)
Explain how function transformations compose in JAX

exercises.prompt: Replace jax.vmap with a manual Python loop over the batch dimension. Does the output change? How does the speed compare?
exercises.hint: Write a for loop that calls the single-example function for each item in the batch. Use jnp.stack to combine results. Compare timing with and without vmap.

exercises.prompt: Stack jax.vmap and jax.jit in different orders: jit(vmap(f)) vs. vmap(jit(f)). Is there a performance difference?
exercises.hint: Wrap the loss function both ways and time 100 training steps each. Check if JAX produces the same XLA computation graph for both.

labs.title: MLX (Apple Silicon)
labs.tagline: Running on Apple Silicon GPU via MLX. Unified memory, lazy evaluation.
labs.description:
MLX is Apple's framework for machine learning on Apple Silicon. It uses unified memory — the same physical RAM is shared between CPU and GPU, so there's no data transfer overhead.

The API looks like PyTorch (modules, parameters, optimizers), but computation is lazy: operations build a graph that's only evaluated when you call mx.eval(). This lets the framework fuse operations and optimize memory access patterns.

If you have a Mac with M1/M2/M3/M4, this lab runs on your GPU with zero configuration.
learning_objectives: Implement a transformer on Apple Silicon using MLX
Explain unified memory and lazy evaluation benefits
Compare MLX's API with PyTorch's module system

exercises.prompt: Remove mx.eval() calls from the training loop and add one at the very end. What happens to memory usage and why?
exercises.hint: Without eval(), MLX builds up a lazy computation graph. Memory grows because no intermediate results are freed until evaluation forces computation.

exercises.prompt: Double the embedding dimension and measure the training time per step. Does it scale linearly or quadratically?
exercises.hint: Attention is O(n*d) per head for n tokens and d dimensions. Track time per step with different embedding sizes to see the scaling.

labs.title: MLX Batched
labs.tagline: Mini-batch training on Apple Silicon, same padding and masking as PyTorch batched.
labs.description:
The batched version of the MLX model, with the same padding, masking, and mini-batch training as PyTorch batched (Lab 04).

MLX's lazy evaluation shines here: the framework can see the entire batch computation graph before executing, enabling better memory planning and operation fusion on the Apple GPU.
learning_objectives: Implement batched training with MLX on Apple GPU
Explain how lazy evaluation enables operation fusion
Compare MLX batching with PyTorch and JAX approaches

exercises.prompt: Compare the wall-clock training time of MLX batched vs. PyTorch batched (Lab 04) on your Mac. Which framework is faster and by how much?
exercises.hint: Run both labs for the same number of steps and measure total time. On Apple Silicon, MLX may have an advantage due to unified memory eliminating CPU-GPU transfers.

exercises.prompt: Change the padding token from its current value to a different unused character. Does the model still train correctly?
exercises.hint: The padding token value itself does not matter as long as the attention mask correctly ignores padded positions. Check that the mask logic uses the same token.

labs.title: BPE Tokenizer
labs.tagline: Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch in pure Python, with zero dependencies.
labs.description:
Byte-Pair Encoding is the tokenization algorithm behind GPT, LLaMA, and most modern LLMs. This lab implements it from scratch in pure Python.

BPE starts with individual characters and iteratively merges the most frequent adjacent pair into a new token. After enough merges, common words become single tokens while rare words are split into subword pieces. This balances vocabulary size against sequence length.

The lab trains a BPE tokenizer on the names dataset and uses it with a simple bigram model. Understanding tokenization is essential — it's the first thing that happens to every input and the last thing that happens to every output.
learning_objectives: Implement Byte-Pair Encoding from scratch without dependencies
Explain how BPE balances vocabulary size against sequence length
Analyze tokenization choices and their impact on model performance

exercises.prompt: Train the BPE tokenizer with 50 merges vs. 200 merges. How does the average number of tokens per name change?
exercises.hint: After training, tokenize all names and compute the mean token count. More merges means longer tokens and shorter sequences, but a larger vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Modify the merge selection to pick the second-most-frequent pair instead of the most frequent. How does the resulting vocabulary differ?
exercises.hint: In the merge loop, sort pairs by frequency and select index 1 instead of 0. Compare the final vocabulary and token lengths.

labs.title: Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE)
labs.tagline: Position encoded by rotating Q and K vectors in complex space.
labs.description:
RoPE encodes position information by rotating query and key vectors in complex space. Instead of adding a position embedding to the input, RoPE multiplies Q and K by rotation matrices whose angles depend on position.

The key property: the dot product between rotated Q and K depends only on their relative position, not absolute. This means the model naturally generalizes to longer sequences than it was trained on. RoPE is used by LLaMA, Mistral, Qwen, and most modern open-weight models.

The implementation uses the real-valued form (pairs of dimensions rotated together) rather than complex numbers, matching production implementations.
learning_objectives: Implement Rotary Position Embeddings using real-valued rotation
Explain why relative position encoding enables length generalization
Compare RoPE with absolute position embeddings

exercises.prompt: Change the RoPE base frequency from 10000 to 1000 and to 100000. How does this affect the model's ability to handle different sequence lengths?
exercises.hint: The base frequency controls how quickly the rotation angles change across positions. Lower values create faster rotations, which may lose long-range position information.

exercises.prompt: Disable RoPE entirely by replacing the rotation with an identity operation (multiply by 1). Compare the generated names to see if the model can still learn position without explicit encoding.
exercises.hint: Set the rotation angles to zero so cos=1 and sin=0. The model may still learn some positional patterns from the causal mask alone.

labs.title: Grouped-Query Attention (GQA/MQA)
labs.tagline: From MHA to MQA to GQA: sharing KV heads reduces memory.
labs.description:
Grouped-Query Attention is a simple but powerful optimization: instead of giving every query head its own key-value head, multiple query heads share the same KV head.

Standard multi-head attention (MHA) has equal numbers of Q, K, and V heads. Multi-query attention (MQA) uses just one KV head for all queries. GQA is the middle ground — groups of query heads share a KV head. This reduces KV cache memory (critical for inference) with minimal quality loss.

LLaMA 2 70B, Mistral, and Gemma all use GQA. This lab implements all three variants so you can compare them directly.
learning_objectives: Implement MHA, MQA, and GQA attention variants
Analyze the memory-quality tradeoff of KV head sharing
Compare attention head configurations used in production models

exercises.prompt: Train the model with MHA, MQA, and GQA using the same number of total parameters. Which variant produces the best names after the same number of steps?
exercises.hint: Adjust embedding dimensions so total parameter count is similar. Compare loss curves and generated output quality across all three variants.

exercises.prompt: Print the KV cache size (in number of elements) for each variant during generation. What is the memory ratio between MHA and MQA?
exercises.hint: Count the number of key and value tensors stored. MQA uses 1 KV head vs. n_heads for MHA, so the ratio should match the number of query heads.

labs.title: KV Cache
labs.tagline: The single most important inference optimization.
labs.description:
During autoregressive generation, each new token attends to all previous tokens. Without caching, this means recomputing K and V for the entire sequence at every step — O(n²) total work for n tokens.

The KV cache stores previously computed key and value tensors and appends new ones incrementally. Each generation step only computes K and V for the new token, then concatenates with the cache. This reduces per-step work from O(n) to O(1) for the projection, though attention itself is still O(n).

Every production LLM serving system uses KV caching. It's the difference between seconds and minutes for long sequences.
learning_objectives: Implement incremental KV caching for autoregressive generation
Analyze the complexity reduction from O(T^3) to O(T^2)
Explain why KV caching is the most important inference optimization

exercises.prompt: Disable the KV cache and time the generation of a 50-character name with and without caching. What is the speedup ratio?
exercises.hint: Without cache, each step recomputes all keys and values from scratch. Time both versions and divide. The ratio should grow roughly linearly with sequence length.

exercises.prompt: Print the shape of the cached key and value tensors at each generation step. Verify they grow by exactly one position per step.
exercises.hint: After each step, print cache_k.shape and cache_v.shape. The sequence dimension should increment by 1 each time.

labs.title: Sampling Strategies
labs.tagline: Five strategies producing completely different output from the same model.
labs.description:
The model outputs logits — raw scores for every token in the vocabulary. How you convert logits to a token choice dramatically affects output quality.

This lab implements five strategies: greedy (always pick the highest), temperature scaling (sharpen or flatten the distribution), top-k (only consider the k most likely), top-p/nucleus (only consider tokens whose cumulative probability reaches p), and min-p (only consider tokens above a fraction of the top token's probability).

The same model produces boring repetitive output with greedy sampling and creative diverse output with temperature + top-p. Understanding sampling is essential for anyone building LLM applications.
learning_objectives: Implement five sampling strategies: greedy, temperature, top-k, top-p, min-p
Analyze how each strategy affects output diversity and quality
Design sampling configurations for different use cases

exercises.prompt: Generate 20 names with temperature=0.1 and then with temperature=2.0. Count how many unique names you get in each case.
exercises.hint: Low temperature makes the distribution peaked (repetitive output), high temperature makes it flat (diverse but potentially nonsensical). Count duplicates in each batch.

exercises.prompt: Combine top-k=5 with top-p=0.9 in the same sampling call. Which filter is more restrictive in practice? Print the number of candidates remaining after each filter.
exercises.hint: Apply top-k first, then top-p to the remaining candidates. Print len(candidates) after each step to see which one eliminates more options.

labs.title: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
labs.tagline: Freeze the model, inject tiny low-rank matrices, and train only those.
labs.description:
LoRA is the most popular parameter-efficient fine-tuning method. Instead of updating all model weights, you freeze them and inject small low-rank matrices (A and B) alongside each target layer.

The original weight matrix W stays frozen. A new path x → A → B (where A and B have much smaller dimensions) learns the task-specific adaptation. At inference, you can merge W + BA into a single matrix with zero overhead.

This lab shows LoRA applied to the attention projections. With rank 4, you train ~1% of the original parameters while matching full fine-tuning quality on this task.
learning_objectives: Implement low-rank adaptation matrices for parameter-efficient fine-tuning
Explain how LoRA achieves full fine-tuning quality with ~1% parameters
Evaluate when to use LoRA vs. full fine-tuning

exercises.prompt: Change the LoRA rank from 4 to 1 and to 16. Compare the final loss and generated names for each rank. At what rank does quality stop improving?
exercises.hint: Lower rank means fewer trainable parameters and faster training, but potentially worse adaptation. Track loss convergence and output quality for each rank.

exercises.prompt: Apply LoRA to only the value projection instead of all attention projections. Does the model still adapt successfully?
exercises.hint: Modify which layers receive LoRA adapters. The value projection often captures the most task-specific information, so it may be sufficient alone.

labs.title: PyTorch Quantized
labs.tagline: Compress a trained model from FP32 to INT8.
labs.description:
INT8 quantization compresses model weights from 32-bit floats to 8-bit integers — a 4× reduction in model size and memory bandwidth.

The process: train normally in FP32, then for each weight matrix, compute a scale factor that maps the float range to [-128, 127]. At inference, multiply the int8 weights by the scale to approximate the original floats. The approximation error is small because neural network weights tend to cluster near zero.

This is how production models run on edge devices, phones, and cost-efficient servers. The lab trains, quantizes, and compares output quality before and after compression.
learning_objectives: Implement post-training INT8 quantization with scale factors
Analyze quantization error and its impact on model output
Evaluate the size-accuracy tradeoff of weight compression

exercises.prompt: Quantize to INT4 instead of INT8 by mapping weights to the range [-8, 7]. How much worse is the generated output compared to INT8?
exercises.hint: Change the scale factor to map to 4-bit range. Compare generated names from FP32, INT8, and INT4 models to see the quality degradation.

exercises.prompt: Print the maximum absolute quantization error for each weight matrix. Which layer has the largest error and why?
exercises.hint: Compute abs(original_weight - dequantized_weight).max() for each layer. Layers with wider weight distributions lose more precision.

labs.title: Text Diffusion
labs.tagline: Names emerge from pure noise through iterative unmasking.
labs.description:
Instead of generating left-to-right, masked diffusion starts from a sequence of all [MASK] tokens and iteratively reveals them. At each step, the model predicts what's behind every mask, and the most confident predictions are unmasked.

This is the text equivalent of image diffusion models like Stable Diffusion. The key difference from autoregressive models: the generation order is determined by model confidence, not left-to-right position. The model might unmask the middle of a name before the beginning.

The lab uses PyTorch for tensors and autograd, but with a fundamentally different training objective (predict masked tokens) and generation process (iterative unmasking with confidence-based remasking).
learning_objectives: Implement masked diffusion for non-autoregressive text generation
Compare confidence-based unmasking with left-to-right generation
Analyze the tradeoffs between diffusion and autoregressive approaches

exercises.prompt: Change the number of diffusion steps from the default to just 2 steps. How does the quality of generated names compare to using 10 or 20 steps?
exercises.hint: Fewer steps means the model must unmask more tokens per step with less confidence. Watch for garbled names when steps are too few.

exercises.prompt: Modify the unmasking schedule to always unmask from left to right instead of by confidence. Does this make the output better or worse?
exercises.hint: Replace the confidence-based selection with positional ordering. This makes it behave more like autoregressive generation but within the diffusion framework.

labs.title: Speculative Decoding
labs.tagline: A small draft model guesses ahead, a larger target model verifies.
labs.description:
Speculative decoding uses two models: a small, fast draft model proposes several tokens at once, then the large target model verifies them all in a single forward pass.

The key insight: verifying N tokens in parallel costs about the same as generating 1 token (because the target model processes them as a batch). If the draft model guesses correctly most of the time, you get N tokens for the cost of ~1. When it guesses wrong, you reject and fall back — the output distribution is mathematically identical to the target model alone.

This is a lossless speedup: same output quality, fewer forward passes. Used in production by vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, and others.
learning_objectives: Implement draft-and-verify speculative decoding
Prove that speculative decoding produces identical output distributions
Analyze the speedup as a function of draft model acceptance rate

exercises.prompt: Increase the number of draft tokens from the default to double and triple. At what point does the acceptance rate drop enough to negate the speedup?
exercises.hint: More draft tokens means more speculative work, but the probability of all being accepted decreases exponentially. Track accepted/proposed ratio.

exercises.prompt: Make the draft model intentionally worse by reducing its size. At what point is speculative decoding slower than just using the target model alone?
exercises.hint: A very small draft model has low acceptance rates. Measure total forward passes for both approaches to find the crossover point.

labs.title: Tiled Attention (FlashAttention)
labs.tagline: Three attention implementations producing identical outputs. The difference is memory trips.
labs.description:
FlashAttention restructures the attention computation to minimize memory transfers between fast SRAM and slow HBM (main GPU memory).

Standard attention materializes the full N×N attention matrix in HBM — that's O(N²) memory. FlashAttention processes attention in tiles that fit in SRAM, accumulating results with online softmax (the log-sum-exp trick). The output is mathematically identical, but memory usage drops from O(N²) to O(N).

This lab implements three versions — naive, standard, and tiled — so you can verify they produce the same output while understanding why the tiled version is faster on real hardware.
learning_objectives: Implement naive, standard, and tiled (FlashAttention) attention
Explain the online softmax trick using log-sum-exp
Analyze why tiled attention reduces memory from O(N^2) to O(N)

exercises.prompt: Change the tile size in the tiled attention implementation. What is the smallest tile that still produces numerically identical output to standard attention?
exercises.hint: Try tile sizes of 1, 2, 4, 8, etc. Compare outputs using np.allclose with a tight tolerance. Tile size affects performance but not correctness.

exercises.prompt: Add a counter to track the number of memory reads and writes in each attention implementation. How do the counts compare between naive and tiled?
exercises.hint: Count each array access as a memory operation. Tiled attention does more total arithmetic but fewer memory round-trips, which is faster on real hardware.

labs.title: Paged KV Cache (PagedAttention)
labs.tagline: Virtual memory paging applied to KV caches.
labs.description:
PagedAttention applies the operating system's virtual memory concept to KV caches. Instead of allocating one contiguous block per sequence, KV data is stored in fixed-size pages that can be anywhere in physical memory.

This solves the fragmentation problem: when sequences have different lengths and lifetimes, contiguous allocation wastes memory on internal fragmentation. Paging eliminates this waste, enabling ~2-4× more concurrent sequences in the same memory budget.

This is the core innovation behind vLLM. The lab implements it with the same pure-Python scalar autograd as Lab 01, making the memory management logic explicit and inspectable.
learning_objectives: Implement virtual memory paging for KV cache management
Analyze memory fragmentation in contiguous vs. paged allocation
Explain how PagedAttention enables 2-4x more concurrent sequences

exercises.prompt: Change the page size from the default to half and double. How does this affect memory fragmentation and the number of page table lookups?
exercises.hint: Smaller pages reduce internal fragmentation but increase page table overhead. Print the total allocated vs. actually used memory for each page size.

exercises.prompt: Simulate 10 concurrent sequences of varying lengths, then free half of them. Print the memory map before and after freeing to visualize fragmentation.
exercises.hint: Track which pages are allocated to which sequences. After freeing, contiguous allocation leaves gaps while paged allocation can reuse any freed page.

labs.title: Soft Thinking
labs.tagline: Pass a probability-weighted blend forward, preserving information.
labs.description:
Standard autoregressive models collapse the output distribution to a single token via argmax or sampling, then embed that token for the next step. Soft thinking instead computes a weighted average of all token embeddings, weighted by the output probabilities.

This "concept token" preserves the model's uncertainty: if the model is 60% sure of 'a' and 40% sure of 'e', the concept token is a blend of both embeddings. No information is discarded. The model can then use this richer signal for subsequent predictions.

The tradeoff: concept tokens are more expensive to compute (a matrix multiply with the full vocabulary) and the model must learn to interpret blended inputs.
learning_objectives: Implement probability-weighted concept tokens as model input
Explain how soft tokens preserve uncertainty across generation steps
Evaluate the compute-information tradeoff of concept tokens

exercises.prompt: Apply a temperature of 0.5 to the probability distribution before computing concept tokens. How does sharpening the distribution affect generation quality?
exercises.hint: Lower temperature makes concept tokens closer to hard one-hot tokens. Compare the generated names with temperature 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 for concept token computation.

exercises.prompt: Print the entropy of the concept token distribution at each generation step. Does the model become more confident as it generates more characters?
exercises.hint: Compute -sum(p * log(p)) for the distribution at each step. Lower entropy means the model is more certain about the next token.

labs.title: Soft Training
labs.tagline: Use concept tokens during training too.
labs.description:
Soft thinking (Lab 17) uses concept tokens at inference but trains with hard (one-hot) tokens. This creates a train-test mismatch: the model never sees blended inputs during training.

Soft training fixes this with a scheduled curriculum. Early in training, inputs are mostly hard tokens. As training progresses, the model gradually receives more concept tokens as input. By the end, it's fully trained on its own soft outputs.

The curriculum schedule (linear ramp from 0% to 100% soft inputs) is critical — starting with all-soft inputs from the beginning doesn't work because the model's early predictions are random noise.
learning_objectives: Implement a curriculum schedule transitioning from hard to soft inputs
Explain the train-test mismatch problem and how soft training solves it
Design curriculum schedules for different convergence behaviors

exercises.prompt: Change the curriculum schedule from linear to exponential (e.g., soft_ratio = min(1, exp(step/total - 1))). Does faster or slower transition to soft inputs work better?
exercises.hint: Exponential schedules spend more time in the hard-token regime early on. Compare final loss and generation quality against the linear schedule.

exercises.prompt: Start training with 100% soft inputs from step 1 (no curriculum). Verify that training fails or produces poor results, confirming why the curriculum is needed.
exercises.hint: Without curriculum, early soft tokens are random noise since the model has not learned yet. Watch the loss curve to see if it converges at all.

labs.title: Disaggregated Serving
labs.tagline: Prefill and decode on separate workers.
labs.description:
LLM inference has two phases with very different compute profiles: prefill (process the entire prompt, compute-bound) and decode (generate tokens one at a time, memory-bound).

When both run on the same GPU, long prefills block short decodes, causing latency spikes. Disaggregated serving splits them onto separate workers: prefill workers process prompts and send KV caches to decode workers via a transfer mechanism.

This eliminates head-of-line blocking and lets you scale each phase independently. The lab simulates this with threads and queues, showing the scheduling and KV cache transfer logic used by production systems like Mooncake and DistServe.
learning_objectives: Implement prefill/decode disaggregation with thread-based workers
Analyze the compute profiles of prefill vs. decode phases
Design a scheduling strategy that eliminates head-of-line blocking

exercises.prompt: Add a third worker type that handles both prefill and decode. Compare the latency distribution against the disaggregated setup with 10 concurrent requests.
exercises.hint: A combined worker processes requests sequentially, so long prefills block short decodes. Measure p50 and p99 latency for both architectures.

exercises.prompt: Simulate a burst of 50 requests arriving at once. How does the queue depth grow for prefill vs. decode workers, and which becomes the bottleneck?
exercises.hint: Add logging to print queue sizes at each step. Prefill is compute-bound and usually slower per request, so its queue should grow faster under burst load.

labs.title: Self-Improving Model
labs.tagline: The model generates data, scores it, and retrains on its own best outputs.
labs.description:
A model that improves itself through iterative self-training. Each round: generate candidate names, score them with a verifiable quality function, keep only the best, and retrain on the mix of real data plus self-generated data.

This is the core pattern behind STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner), self-rewarding language models, and Karpathy's autoresearch project. The key insight: keeping only verified good outputs prevents the collapse that naive self-training causes. A bigram-statistics scorer acts as the verifier, making the lab fully self-contained — no external LLM needed.

Watch the quality metric improve across rounds as the model bootstraps from its own best generations.
learning_objectives: Implement the generate-score-filter-retrain self-improvement loop
Explain why filtering is essential to prevent self-training collapse
Compare self-improved output quality against the baseline

exercises.prompt: Remove the quality filter and keep ALL self-generated names for retraining. Does the model still improve, or does it collapse?
exercises.hint: Without filtering, the model retrains on its own mistakes, amplifying errors. Watch the quality metric — it should degrade rather than improve.

exercises.prompt: Change the mix ratio from 30% self-generated to 80% self-generated data. How does this affect the speed and stability of improvement?
exercises.hint: More self-generated data means faster adaptation but higher risk of distribution drift. Track quality over rounds for both settings.

labs.title: Evolutionary Self-Improvement
labs.tagline: A population of model variants competes, mutates, and evolves.
labs.description:
Instead of improving one model's outputs (Lab 23), this lab improves the model itself through population-based evolution. A population of tiny GPT variants — each with different hyperparameters — compete for survival across generations.

Each generation: train all models briefly, evaluate fitness (validation loss), keep the best, replace the rest with mutated copies of the winners. Children can inherit trained weights from parents (the 'exploit' step) while exploring new hyperparameter settings (the 'explore' step).

This implements Population Based Training (PBT), the same pattern behind Karpathy's autoresearch and DeepMind's FunSearch. Evolution discovers good configurations that a single random guess would likely miss.
learning_objectives: Implement population-based training with selection and mutation
Analyze the exploit-explore tradeoff in evolutionary optimization
Compare evolved configurations against random baselines

exercises.prompt: Double the population size from 8 to 16. Does evolution find better configurations, and how does the compute cost scale?
exercises.hint: Larger populations explore more configurations per generation but cost more compute. Track both the best fitness and total training time.

exercises.prompt: Add weight inheritance even when the architecture changes, by copying overlapping parameters. Does this speed up evolution?
exercises.hint: When n_embd changes, copy the intersection of the weight matrices. The inherited knowledge may give the child a head start on training.
